title: Word search using Trie
description: Find words in board using Trie

examples.explanation: Board: a b / c d. Words: ab, ac, bd, abcd. Start (0,0) 'a': DFS: path='a' -> neighbor (0,1) 'b' gives 'ab' (found), neighbor (1,0) 'c' gives 'ac' (found). Start (0,1) 'b': DFS: path='b' -> neighbor (1,1) 'd' gives 'bd' (found). Start (1,0) 'c': DFS: path='c' -> neighbor (0,0) 'a' gives 'ca' (not in list), neighbor (1,1) 'd' gives 'cd' (not). Start (1,1) 'd': DFS: path='d' -> neighbor (0,1) 'b' gives 'db' (not), neighbor (1,0) 'c' gives 'dc' (not). 'abcd' not found as path length >2. Results: ab, ac, bd.

Board 4x4. Words: oath, pea, eat, rain. Start (0,0) 'o': DFS explores paths: o->a (0,1)->t (1,1)->h (2,1) gives 'oath' (found). Start (1,0) 'e': DFS: e->a (1,2)->t (1,1) gives 'eat' (found). 'pea' not found (no 'p' adjacent to 'e' and 'a'). 'rain' not found (no valid path). Results: oath, eat.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from each cell to explore all possible paths, checking if formed strings match any word in the list. Use backtracking to avoid revisiting cells and prune paths that exceed word lengths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Find all words from a given list that can be formed by adjacent letters in a 2D board using DFS backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete word search problem solved via brute force DFS. The intuition is to treat the board as a graph where each cell is a node connected to its four neighbors (up, down, left, right). Starting from every cell, we perform DFS to explore all possible paths, building strings along the way. We check if the current path forms a word in the given list. Backtracking is crucial: we mark cells as visited during exploration and unmark them when backtracking to allow reuse in other paths. We also prune paths that exceed the maximum word length to avoid unnecessary exploration. The algorithm works because it exhaustively checks all possible sequences starting from each cell, ensuring no valid word is missed. Step-by-step: 1) For each cell (i,j) in the board, call DFS with starting path = board[i][j]. 2) In DFS: if current string is in word list, add to results; if path length exceeds max word length, return; mark cell as visited; for each neighbor (within bounds and not visited), append its letter to path and recurse; unmark cell. 3) Use a set for results to avoid duplicates. Time: O(m*n*4^L) where m,n are board dimensions, L is max word length (each DFS can explore 4^L paths). Space: O(L) for recursion depth and visited tracking. Connection to L1: This integrates the DFS subroutine from L1 with board iteration and result collection. Common mistakes: forgetting to unmark cells (causes missed words), not pruning by length (causes TLE), using list for results without deduplication. Real code pattern: def findWords(board, words): results=set(); max_len=max(len(w) for w in words); m,n=len(board),len(board[0]); visited=[[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def dfs(i,j,path): if path in words: results.add(path); if len(path)>max_len: return; visited[i][j]=True; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj=i+di,j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n and not visited[ni][nj]: dfs(ni,nj,path+board[ni][nj]); visited[i][j]=False; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): dfs(i,j,board[i][j]); return list(results).

examples.trace: Board: a b / c d. Words: ab, ac, bd, abcd. Start (0,0) 'a': DFS: path='a' -> neighbor (0,1) 'b' gives 'ab' (found), neighbor (1,0) 'c' gives 'ac' (found). Start (0,1) 'b': DFS: path='b' -> neighbor (1,1) 'd' gives 'bd' (found). Start (1,0) 'c': DFS: path='c' -> neighbor (0,0) 'a' gives 'ca' (not in list), neighbor (1,1) 'd' gives 'cd' (not). Start (1,1) 'd': DFS: path='d' -> neighbor (0,1) 'b' gives 'db' (not), neighbor (1,0) 'c' gives 'dc' (not). 'abcd' not found as path length >2. Results: ab, ac, bd.

Board 4x4. Words: oath, pea, eat, rain. Start (0,0) 'o': DFS explores paths: o->a (0,1)->t (1,1)->h (2,1) gives 'oath' (found). Start (1,0) 'e': DFS: e->a (1,2)->t (1,1) gives 'eat' (found). 'pea' not found (no 'p' adjacent to 'e' and 'a'). 'rain' not found (no valid path). Results: oath, eat.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Backtracking from a Cell
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given cell to explore all possible paths, building strings and checking against a word list.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is to recursively explore all paths starting from a specific cell, treating the board as a graph where movement is allowed to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right). At each step, we append the current cell's letter to the path string. We check if the current path matches any word in the given list and add it to results if so. To avoid infinite loops and reuse of cells within a single path, we mark the current cell as visited before recursing to neighbors and unmark it after returning (backtracking). This allows the cell to be used in other paths. We also prune the search by stopping if the path length exceeds the maximum word length, as no valid word can be formed beyond that. The algorithm works because it systematically explores all possible sequences of adjacent letters starting from the given cell. Step-by-step: 1) Input: board, starting coordinates (i,j), current path string, visited matrix, word set, max length, results set. 2) Base cases: if path in word set, add to results; if len(path) > max length, return. 3) Mark cell (i,j) as visited. 4) For each neighbor (up, down, left, right) that is within bounds and not visited: recursively call DFS with path + neighbor's letter. 5) Unmark cell (i,j). Time: O(4^L) in worst case as each call can branch to 4 neighbors up to depth L. Space: O(L) for recursion stack and visited tracking per path. Connection to L0: This DFS function is called from each cell in the board in L0. Common mistakes: not unmarking cells (breaks backtracking), missing bounds checks, incorrect neighbor coordinates. Real code pattern: def dfs(i,j,path): if path in words: results.add(path); if len(path)>max_len: return; visited[i][j]=True; for di,dj in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: ni,nj=i+di,j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n and not visited[ni][nj]: dfs(ni,nj,path+board[ni][nj]); visited[i][j]=False.

examples.input: Start at (0,0) in board [['a','b'],['c','d']], initial path='a', words={'ab','ac'}, max_len=2
examples.trace: DFS from (0,0) with path='a': path='a' not in words, len=1<=2. Mark (0,0) visited. Neighbors: (0,1) 'b' -> recurse with path='ab': path='ab' in words, add; len=2<=2, mark (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) visited skip, (1,1) 'd' -> path='abd' len=3>2 return, unmark (0,1). Neighbor (1,0) 'c' -> recurse with path='ac': path='ac' in words, add; len=2<=2, mark (1,0), neighbors: (0,0) visited skip, (1,1) 'd' -> path='acd' len=3>2 return, unmark (1,0). Unmark (0,0). Results: ab, ac.

examples.input: Start at (1,1) in board [['x','y'],['z','w']], initial path='w', words={'wz','wy'}, max_len=2
examples.trace: DFS from (1,1) with path='w': not in words, len=1<=2. Mark (1,1). Neighbors: (0,1) 'y' -> path='wy' not in words, len=2<=2, mark (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) 'x' -> path='wyx' len=3>2 return, (1,1) visited skip, unmark (0,1). Neighbor (1,0) 'z' -> path='wz' in words, add; len=2<=2, mark (1,0), neighbors: (0,0) 'x' -> path='wzx' len=3>2 return, (1,1) visited skip, unmark (1,0). Neighbor (0,1) already processed, (1,0) processed. Unmark (1,1). Results: wz.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Mark and unmark cells during DFS to avoid reuse in the same path while allowing reuse across different paths.
ladders.explanation: This core logic enables efficient exploration without infinite loops. The intuition is that in a DFS path, we cannot revisit a cell because that would create cycles (e.g., 'ababa' from alternating cells). However, across different DFS calls (starting from different cells or in different branches), cells can be reused because each path is independent. We achieve this by marking a cell as visited when we enter it in the current DFS call and unmarking it when we exit (backtrack). This ensures that within a single recursive exploration, the cell is not revisited, but once we return to a previous state, it becomes available for other paths. The algorithm works by maintaining a visited matrix (boolean 2D array) same size as the board. At the start of DFS from a cell, we set visited[i][j] = true. Then, when exploring neighbors, we only proceed if visited[ni][nj] is false. After processing all neighbors, we set visited[i][j] = false before returning. This pattern is classic backtracking. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize visited all false. 2) In DFS: set visited[i][j]=true. 3) Recurse to neighbors with visited check. 4) Set visited[i][j]=false. Time: O(1) per cell for marking/unmarking. Space: O(m*n) for visited matrix, but in practice shared across calls. Connection to L1: This visited tracking is embedded in the DFS subroutine to prevent cycles. Common mistakes: forgetting to unmark (causes cells to remain visited and block other paths), using a local visited copy per call (wastes space). Real code pattern: visited=[[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def dfs(i,j,...): visited[i][j]=True; ... recurse ...; visited[i][j]=False.

examples.input: Board 2x2, start DFS from (0,0), show visited states during exploration of path 'a->b->d'
examples.output: Visited matrix changes: start all false; at (0,0) true; at (0,1) true; at (1,1) true; backtrack to (0,1) false; backtrack to (0,0) false.
examples.trace: Initial visited: [[F,F],[F,F]]. DFS at (0,0): set visited[0][0]=T. Explore neighbor (0,1): check visited[0][1]=F, recurse. In that call: set visited[0][1]=T, explore neighbor (1,1): visited[1][1]=F, recurse. In that call: set visited[1][1]=T, path='abd', then return and set visited[1][1]=F. Back in (0,1) call: after exploring, set visited[0][1]=F. Back in (0,0) call: after exploring, set visited[0][0]=F. Final visited: all F.

examples.input: Board 1x3 ['a','b','c'], start DFS from (0,0), explore path 'a->b' and then 'a->c', show visited
examples.output: Visited ensures 'a' not reused in same path but available for second path.
examples.trace: Initial visited: [[F,F,F]]. DFS at (0,0): set visited[0][0]=T. Explore neighbor (0,1): visited[0][1]=F, recurse: set visited[0][1]=T, path='ab', return and set visited[0][1]=F. Explore neighbor (0,2): visited[0][2]=F, recurse: set visited[0][2]=T, path='ac', return and set visited[0][2]=F. Set visited[0][0]=F. Final all F.

testCases.input: Board 1x1, start at (0,0), mark and unmark
testCases.expected: Visited: start F, during call T, after call F

testCases.input: Board 2x2, start at (0,0), explore all neighbors
testCases.expected: Visited: (0,0) T during, then F after

testCases.input: Board 2x2, start at (0,0), path to (0,1) and back
testCases.expected: Visited: (0,0) and (0,1) T during, both F after

testCases.input: Board 3x3, start at (1,1), explore in all directions
testCases.expected: Visited: center T during, neighbors T during their calls, all F after

testCases.input: Board 2x2, two separate DFS calls from (0,0) and (1,1)
Board 1x3, start at (0,0), move right twice
testCases.expected: Visited: cells T sequentially, all F after
Visited: (0,0) remains T, blocking other paths
Visited marks up to 2 cells, all F after

testCases.input: Board 2x2, start at (0,0), neighbor out of bounds

ladders.title: Building Block — Exploring Adjacent Cells in a Grid
ladders.desc: Generate all valid neighbor coordinates (up, down, left, right) from a given cell in a 2D grid.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for traversing the board in DFS. The intuition is that in a word search, we can move to adjacent cells horizontally or vertically, but not diagonally. From any cell (i,j), the possible moves are to (i-1,j) [up], (i+1,j) [down], (i,j-1) [left], and (i,j+1) [right]. However, we must ensure these coordinates are within the bounds of the grid (0 <= i < m, 0 <= j < n) to avoid index errors. This is a fundamental graph traversal step where the grid is treated as a graph with edges between adjacent cells. The algorithm works by predefining direction vectors: directions = [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)] representing (delta_i, delta_j). For each direction, compute new coordinates (ni, nj) = (i + di, j + dj). Check if ni and nj are within bounds: 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n. If valid, (ni,nj) is a neighbor. This ensures we only explore existing cells. Step-by-step: 1) Input: current coordinates (i,j), grid dimensions m,n. 2) Define directions list. 3) For each (di,dj) in directions: ni = i + di; nj = j + dj. 4) If 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n: output (ni,nj). Time: O(1) per cell since only 4 directions. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This neighbor generation is used in the DFS to explore next cells, combined with visited checks. Common mistakes: missing bounds checks (causes index errors), incorrect direction vectors (e.g., including diagonals), off-by-one errors in bounds. Real code pattern: directions = [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n: # valid neighbor.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking String Membership in a Set
ladders.desc: Determine if a given string exists in a collection of words efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is used to check if the current path forms a valid word. The intuition is that we need a fast way to test whether a string is in the list of target words. Using a set data structure provides O(1) average-time membership checks, which is crucial for performance since we check many paths. A set is a collection of unique elements that allows constant-time lookups based on hashing. In contrast, a list would require O(N) linear search per check, which is inefficient. The algorithm works by converting the list of words into a set at the start. Then, during DFS, we check if the current path string is in the set using the 'in' operator. If it is, we add it to results. Step-by-step: 1) Input: list of words. 2) Convert to set: word_set = set(words). 3) To check a string s: if s in word_set: # found. Time: O(1) per check on average. Space: O(W) where W is number of words. Connection to L3: This check is performed during neighbor exploration in DFS to identify valid words. Common mistakes: using list for membership (slow), not handling duplicates in word list (set automatically deduplicates), case sensitivity issues. Real code pattern: words = ['cat','dog']; word_set = set(words); s = 'cat'; if s in word_set: print('found').

examples.trace: Set contains 'apple', so 'apple' in set returns True.
'hell' is not in the set, so check returns False.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Grid Representation and Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Understanding how to represent a 2D board in code and access adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: how to construct a string dynamically. In the word search problem, we start with an initial character from a cell and append neighbors' characters as we traverse. The intuition is that a string is immutable in many languages, so each append creates a new string. We build the path incrementally: start with an empty string or initial char, and for each step, add the next character to the end. In DFS, this is done recursively: we pass the current path to a function call, and when recursing to a neighbor, we create a new string by concatenating the current path with the neighbor's character. For example, if path = 'ab' and next char = 'c', new_path = path + 'c' = 'abc'. This is straightforward but important for understanding how the path grows. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize path = '' or first character. 2) In a loop or recursion: new_path = path + next_char. 3) Use new_path for next step. Time: O(L) per concatenation where L is string length, as it creates a new copy. Space: O(L) for the new string. Connection to L4: This string building is used to create the path that is checked for membership in the word set. Common mistakes: modifying string in place (not possible in immutable languages), using list for efficiency (can be optimized later). Real code pattern: path = 'a'; next_char = 'b'; new_path = path + next_char; # 'ab'.

examples.trace: Initial: path='c'. Append 'a': new_path='c'+'a'='ca'. Append 't': new_path='ca'+'t'='cat'.
Initial: path='h'. Append 'i': new_path='h'+'i'='hi'.

approaches.name: Trie with DFS Backtracking O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Build a Trie from the word list, then perform DFS from each cell, traversing the Trie to check prefixes and find words. Backtrack to explore all paths and mark visited cells.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Trie and DFS Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all words from a list in a character board using Trie and DFS backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all words from a given list that exist in a 2D board of characters. Words can be formed by sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically), and each cell can be used at most once per word. The challenge is to efficiently search for multiple words without redundant work. The intuition behind using a Trie is to store all words in a prefix tree, allowing us to quickly check if a path in the board corresponds to any word or prefix in the list. This avoids exploring paths that cannot lead to valid words. The algorithm involves two main phases: building a Trie from the word list, and performing DFS from each cell in the board while traversing the Trie. During DFS, we mark cells as visited to prevent reuse, and backtrack to explore all possible paths. When we reach a node in the Trie that marks the end of a word, we add that word to the result set. The time complexity is O(m*n*4^L), where m and n are board dimensions, and L is the maximum word length, due to DFS exploring up to 4 directions per cell. Space complexity is O(T + L), where T is the total characters in the Trie and L is the recursion depth. This approach is efficient because it prunes invalid paths early using the Trie. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the DFS backtracking subroutine with Trie traversal. Common mistakes include not marking cells as visited correctly, forgetting to backtrack, or not handling duplicate words. Real code pattern: Build Trie with insert method, DFS function that takes board position and Trie node, checks bounds and visited, moves to child node, recurses to neighbors, backtracks visited, and adds word if node.is_end is true.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build Trie from words: root -> a -> b (end), a -> c (end), b -> d (end), c -> d (end). Step 2: Start DFS from (0,0) cell 'a': move to node 'a' in Trie, mark visited, check neighbors: right (0,1) 'b' is child of 'a', move to node 'b', it's end, add 'ab', backtrack. Down (1,0) 'c' is not child of 'a', stop. Step 3: From (0,1) 'b': move to node 'b', down (1,1) 'd' is child, move to node 'd', end, add 'bd', backtrack. Step 4: From (1,0) 'c': move to node 'c', right (1,1) 'd' is child, move to node 'd', end, add 'cd', backtrack. Step 5: From (1,1) 'd': no words start with 'd', skip. Result: ['ab','bd','cd'].

Step 1: Build Trie: root -> o -> a -> t -> h (end), p -> e -> a (end), e -> a -> t (end), r -> a -> i -> n (end). Step 2: DFS from (0,0) 'o': move to node 'o', down (1,0) 'e' not child, right (0,1) 'a' is child, move to 'a', down (1,1) 't' is child, move to 't', down (2,1) 'h' is child, move to 'h', end, add 'oath', backtrack. Step 3: From (1,0) 'e': move to node 'e', right (1,1) 'a' is child, move to 'a', right (1,2) 'a' not child, down (2,1) 'h' not child, up (0,1) 'a' not child, backtrack to 'e', down (2,0) 'i' not child, stop. Step 4: From (1,2) 'a': move to node 'a' (from root), right (1,3) 'e' is child of 'a'? No, 'a' child is 't' for 'oath', so stop. Step 5: From (1,3) 'e': move to node 'e', left (1,2) 'a' is child, move to 'a', down (2,2) 'k' not child, up (0,2) 'a' not child, right (1,3) visited, left (1,1) 't' is child, move to 't', end, add 'eat', backtrack. 'pea' and 'rain' not found. Result: ['eat','oath'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Backtracking with Trie Traversal
ladders.desc: DFS function that explores board paths while traversing Trie nodes to find words.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the word search algorithm, responsible for performing depth-first search on the board while simultaneously navigating the Trie data structure. The intuition is to start from a given cell and recursively explore all possible paths (up, down, left, right) that correspond to valid prefixes in the Trie. By marking cells as visited and backtracking, we ensure each cell is used at most once per path, and we can explore all combinations. The algorithm takes as input the current board position (i, j), the current Trie node, and the current path string. It checks if the cell is within bounds and not visited, and if the character matches a child of the current Trie node. If so, it moves to that child node, marks the cell as visited, and recursively calls itself for all four neighboring cells. If the child node marks the end of a word, the path string is added to the results. After exploring all neighbors, it backtracks by unmarking the cell as visited. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(4^L) in the worst case, where L is the maximum word length, as it explores up to 4 branches per recursion level. Space complexity is O(L) for the recursion stack and visited markings. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called from each cell in the board in the full solution. Common mistakes include not checking bounds before accessing the board, forgetting to unmark visited cells, or not handling the case where multiple words share prefixes. Real code pattern: def dfs(i, j, node, path): if not (0 <= i < m and 0 <= j < n) or visited[i][j]: return; char = board[i][j]; if char not in node.children: return; node = node.children[char]; path += char; if node.is_end: add path to results; visited[i][j] = True; for di, dj in directions: dfs(i+di, j+dj, node, path); visited[i][j] = False.

examples.input: Start at (0,0) with board [['a','b'],['c','d']], Trie root with child 'a', path=''
examples.trace: Step 1: At (0,0), char='a', node has child 'a', move to node 'a', path='a', node.is_end=False. Mark visited. Step 2: Explore neighbors: right (0,1) char='b', node 'a' has child 'b', move to node 'b', path='ab', node.is_end=True, add 'ab' to results. Backtrack: unmark (0,1), return to (0,0). Step 3: Explore down (1,0) char='c', node 'a' has no child 'c', return. Step 4: Backtrack: unmark (0,0). Done.

examples.input: Start at (1,0) with board [['o','a'],['e','t']], Trie root with child 'e' -> 'a' -> 't' (end), path=''
examples.trace: Step 1: At (1,0), char='e', node has child 'e', move to node 'e', path='e', is_end=False. Mark visited. Step 2: Explore neighbors: up (0,0) char='o', no child, skip; right (1,1) char='t', node 'e' has child 'a', not 't', skip; down out of bounds; left out of bounds. Step 3: Actually, correct: from 'e', right (1,1) 't' is not child, so stop. Wait, need correct board: assume board as given. Let's trace properly: Start at (1,0) 'e', move to node 'e', path='e'. Mark visited. Neighbors: up (0,0) 'o' not child; right (1,1) 't' not child of 'e' (child is 'a'); down and left out. So no word found. To get 'eat', start at (1,0) 'e', right (1,1) 'a' (if board has 'a'), then down or up to 't'. For simplicity, assume board [['e','a'],['t','x']] and start at (0,0): Step 1: (0,0) 'e', move to node 'e', path='e'. Mark visited. Step 2: right (0,1) 'a', node 'e' has child 'a', move to node 'a', path='ea', is_end=False. Mark visited. Step 3: down (1,1) 'x' not child; left visited; up out; right out. Backtrack to 'a'. Step 4: from 'a', down (1,0) 't', node 'a' has child 't', move to node 't', path='eat', is_end=True, add 'eat'. Backtrack.

testCases.input: board = [['a']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a' end, path=''
board = [['a','b']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'b' end, path=''
board = [['a','b'],['c','d']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'c' end, path=''
board = [['x','y'],['z','w']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a' end, path=''
board = [['a','a'],['a','a']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'a'->'a' end, path=''
board = [['a','b','c']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'b'->'c' end, path=''
board = [['a','b'],['c','d']], start=(1,1), Trie with 'd' end, path=''
board = [['a','b'],['c','d']], start=(0,1), Trie with 'b'->'d' end, path=''
board = [['a','b'],['c','d']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'b'->'d' end, path=''
board = [['a','b'],['c','d']], start=(0,0), Trie with 'a'->'b'->'c' end, path=''

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie-Prefix Matching During DFS
ladders.desc: The technique of using Trie to guide DFS by matching prefixes and pruning invalid paths.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight of combining Trie with DFS to efficiently search for words in a board. The intuition is that a Trie (prefix tree) allows us to store all words in a way that shared prefixes are represented only once. During DFS exploration of the board, at each step, we check if the current path (sequence of characters) corresponds to a prefix in the Trie. If it does, we continue DFS; if not, we stop exploring that path immediately. This pruning is key to efficiency, as it avoids wasting time on paths that cannot form any word from the list. The algorithm involves traversing the Trie node-by-node as we move through the board: starting from the root, for each cell visited, we move to the child node corresponding to the cell's character. If such a child exists, we proceed; otherwise, we backtrack. When we reach a Trie node that marks the end of a word, we record the word. This approach ensures that we only explore paths that are valid prefixes, dramatically reducing the search space compared to checking each path against all words. Time complexity for this logic is O(L) per path for Trie traversal, where L is the path length, but integrated into DFS. Space complexity is O(T) for the Trie. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented within the DFS subroutine to decide whether to continue recursion. Common mistakes include not updating the Trie node correctly during recursion or not resetting it on backtrack. Real code pattern: In DFS, after checking bounds and visited, get char from board[i][j]; if char in node.children: node = node.children[char]; else: return.

examples.input: Trie with words ['cat','car'], current node at root, board path so far 'ca', next char 't'
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. After path 'c'->'a', node is at 'a'. Step 2: Next char is 't'. Check if node 'a' has child 't'. Yes, because 'cat' is in Trie. Step 3: Move to node 't'. Node 't' is end for 'cat', so word found.

examples.input: Trie with words ['dog','dot'], current node at 'd', board path so far 'd', next char 'a'
examples.trace: Step 1: Current node is 'd' (child of root). Step 2: Next char is 'a'. Check if node 'd' has child 'a'. No, because words are 'dog' and 'dot', so children are 'o' only. Step 3: Return early, pruning this path.

testCases.input: Trie words: ['a'], node at root, next char 'a'
Trie words: ['ab'], node at 'a', next char 'b'
Trie words: ['abc'], node at 'a', next char 'x'
Trie words: ['cat','car'], node at 'c'->'a', next char 't'
Trie words: ['cat','car'], node at 'c'->'a', next char 'r'
Trie words: ['cat','car'], node at 'c'->'a', next char 'b'
Trie words: ['test','temp'], node at 't'->'e', next char 's'
Trie words: ['test','temp'], node at 't'->'e', next char 'm'
Trie words: ['hello'], node at 'h'->'e'->'l'->'l', next char 'o'
Trie words: [], node at root, next char 'a'

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure Implementation
ladders.desc: Implementing a Trie (prefix tree) to store and search words efficiently.
ladders.explanation: A Trie is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings, where keys are usually strings. It is particularly useful for problems involving prefixes, like word search, because it allows for efficient prefix queries. The intuition is that each node in the Trie represents a single character of a string, and paths from the root to nodes correspond to prefixes or full words. By sharing common prefixes among words, it saves space and enables fast lookups. The algorithm involves defining a TrieNode class with children (a dictionary mapping characters to child nodes) and a boolean flag is_end to mark the end of a word. The Trie class has methods: insert(word) to add a word by traversing or creating nodes for each character, and search(word) to check if a word exists. For word search, we primarily use insert to build the Trie from the word list, and during DFS, we traverse the Trie without using search explicitly. Time complexity for insert is O(L) per word, where L is word length. Space complexity is O(T), where T is the total number of characters in all words. Connection to L2: The Trie is used to store words and enable prefix matching during DFS. Common mistakes include not initializing children properly, forgetting to set is_end, or not handling duplicate words. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. class Trie: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def insert(word): node = self.root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.is_end = True.

examples.input: Insert words ['cat','car'] into an empty Trie
examples.output: Trie structure: root -> c -> a -> t (end) and a -> r (end)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with root. Insert 'cat': char 'c' not in root.children, create node for 'c', move to it. Char 'a' not in node 'c'.children, create node for 'a', move to it. Char 't' not in node 'a'.children, create node for 't', move to it. Set is_end=True for node 't'. Step 2: Insert 'car': start at root, 'c' exists, move to node 'c'. 'a' exists, move to node 'a'. 'r' not in node 'a'.children, create node for 'r', move to it. Set is_end=True for node 'r'. Done.

examples.input: Insert words ['a','ab','abc'] into an empty Trie
examples.output: Trie structure: root -> a (end) -> b (end) -> c (end)
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 'a': root -> 'a', set is_end=True. Step 2: Insert 'ab': root -> 'a' (exists), move to 'a', 'b' not in children, create 'b', move to it, set is_end=True. Step 3: Insert 'abc': root -> 'a' (exists), move to 'a', 'b' (exists), move to 'b', 'c' not in children, create 'c', move to it, set is_end=True. Note: 'a' remains end for word 'a'.

testCases.expected: root -> a -> b (end) and a -> c (end)
root -> d -> o -> g (end) and o -> t (end); l -> o -> g (end)
root -> h -> e -> l -> l (end) -> o (end)
root with is_end=False? Actually, empty string not typically handled, but if inserted, root.is_end=True for ''
root -> a -> p -> p (end) -> l -> e (end)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS on a 2D Grid with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Performing depth-first search on a grid to explore all paths with backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves traversing a 2D grid (like a board) using depth-first search while implementing backtracking to track visited cells. The intuition is to explore all possible paths from a starting cell by moving to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right), ensuring that no cell is revisited in the same path to avoid cycles. Backtracking is used to mark cells as visited before recursion and unmark them after, allowing the same cell to be used in different paths. This is essential for problems like word search where paths can overlap but cells cannot be reused within a single word. The algorithm uses recursion: from a current cell (i, j), mark it as visited, then recursively call DFS for each of the four neighbors if they are within bounds and not visited. After exploring all neighbors, unmark the cell. This explores all simple paths in the grid. Time complexity is O(4^L) for paths up to length L, as each step can branch into up to 4 directions. Space complexity is O(L) for the recursion stack and visited array. Connection to L3: This DFS operation is enhanced in word search by integrating Trie traversal. Common mistakes include not checking bounds, forgetting to unmark visited, or using incorrect direction arrays. Real code pattern: visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def dfs(i, j, path): if not (0 <= i < m and 0 <= j < n) or visited[i][j]: return; visited[i][j] = True; path += board[i][j]; # process path; for di, dj in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: dfs(i+di, j+dj, path); visited[i][j] = False.

examples.input: Grid 2x2: [['a','b'],['c','d']], start at (0,0), explore all paths up to length 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at (0,0), mark visited, path='a'. Neighbors: right (0,1) not visited, go to (0,1), mark visited, path='ab', length 2, backtrack to (0,0). Step 2: From (0,0), down (1,0) not visited, go to (1,0), mark visited, path='ac', length 2, backtrack to (0,0). Step 3: Unmark (0,0). Paths found: 'a', 'ab', 'ac'. Note: 'a' is path of length 1.

examples.input: Grid 1x3: [['x','y','z']], start at (0,0), explore all paths
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at (0,0), mark visited, path='x'. Neighbors: right (0,1) not visited, go to (0,1), mark visited, path='xy'. Step 2: From (0,1), right (0,2) not visited, go to (0,2), mark visited, path='xyz'. Step 3: Backtrack: unmark (0,2), return to (0,1), unmark (0,1), return to (0,0), unmark (0,0). Paths: 'x', 'xy', 'xyz'.

testCases.expected: paths up to length 2 include all directions

testCases.input: Grid 2x2, start=(0,0), visited all except start

testCases.expected: infinite paths, but with backtracking, finite up to length

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Understanding Tries and Prefix Trees
ladders.desc: Introduction to Trie data structure for storing and retrieving strings.
ladders.explanation: A Trie, also known as a prefix tree, is a fundamental data structure used to store a collection of strings in a way that allows for efficient insertion, deletion, and lookup operations, especially for prefix-based queries. The intuition is to represent strings as paths in a tree, where each node corresponds to a character, and the root represents an empty string. This organization makes it easy to check if a given prefix exists among the stored strings by traversing from the root. For example, if we store words 'cat' and 'car', they share the prefix 'ca', so the Trie will have a common path for 'c' and 'a', branching at 't' and 'r'. This saves space and speeds up searches. The basic operations include: inserting a string by creating nodes for each character if they don't exist, and searching for a string by traversing nodes and checking if the end is marked. Tries are commonly used in autocomplete systems, spell checkers, and problems like word search. Time complexity for insert and search is O(L), where L is the length of the string. Space complexity is O(T), with T being the total characters stored. Connection to L4: Tries build upon this foundation to enhance DFS in word search. Common mistakes include not handling case sensitivity, not marking end of words, or not using dictionaries for children. Real code pattern: Simple class with children dict and is_end flag.

examples.output: Trie with nodes: root -> h -> e -> l -> l -> o (end)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. For 'h': not in children, create node for 'h', move to it. Step 2: For 'e': not in children of 'h', create node for 'e', move to it. Step 3: For 'l': create node, move. Step 4: For 'l': create node, move. Step 5: For 'o': create node, move, set is_end=True.

examples.output: Prefix exists, but not a full word
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. For 'h': exists, move to node 'h'. Step 2: For 'e': exists, move to node 'e'. Step 3: End of search, check is_end at node 'e': False, so 'he' is not a stored word, but it is a prefix.

approaches.name: Trie with Optimized Pruning O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Enhance the Trie approach by removing words from the Trie as they are found to avoid duplicates and pruning Trie nodes with no children to reduce search space. This minimizes redundant traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Trie and Optimized Pruning
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all words from a dictionary in a character board using Trie with pruning to avoid duplicates and reduce search space.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the word search problem using a Trie data structure with optimization techniques. The intuition is that a Trie allows us to efficiently track multiple word prefixes simultaneously during board traversal, while pruning (removing found words and empty nodes) eliminates redundant searches. The algorithm works by: 1) Building a Trie from the dictionary words. 2) Performing DFS from each cell in the m×n board. 3) At each step, checking if the current path forms a valid word in the Trie. 4) When a word is found, adding it to results and removing it from the Trie to prevent duplicates. 5) Pruning Trie nodes that have no children after word removal. 6) Using backtracking with visited tracking. The time complexity is O(m×n×4^L) where L is the maximum word length, as each cell could initiate DFS exploring up to 4^L paths. Space complexity is O(T + L) where T is total characters in Trie and L is recursion depth. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete system where the DFS traversal (L1) is the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not marking visited cells properly (causing infinite loops), forgetting to backtrack visited marks, not handling Trie pruning correctly, and missing edge cases like empty board or dictionary. Real code pattern: Build Trie → DFS from each cell → Check Trie nodes → Add found words → Prune → Backtrack.

examples.trace: 1. Build Trie: root→a→b(word), a→c(word), b→d(word), a→b→c→d(word). 2. Start DFS from (0,0)='a': path='a', Trie node=a. 3. Explore neighbor (0,1)='b': path='ab', Trie node=a→b (word found). Add 'ab' to results, remove 'ab' from Trie (mark b as non-word). 4. Backtrack to (0,0). 5. Explore neighbor (1,0)='c': path='ac', Trie node=a→c (word found). Add 'ac' to results, but 'ac' not in board path (needs adjacent c from a). Actually 'ac' not possible from (0,0) as c is diagonal. 6. Continue DFS from all cells: From (0,1)='b': path='b', Trie node=b. Explore (1,1)='d': path='bd', Trie node=b→d (word found). Add 'bd'. Remove 'bd' from Trie. 7. 'abcd' not found as path not contiguous. 8. Final result: ['ab','bd'].

1. Build Trie with words: oath, pea, eat, rain. 2. Start DFS from (0,0)='o': path='o', Trie node=o. 3. Explore (1,0)='e': path='oe' not in Trie, backtrack. 4. Explore (0,1)='a': path='oa', Trie node=o→a. 5. Explore (0,2)='a': path='oaa' not in Trie, backtrack. 6. Explore (1,1)='t': path='oat', Trie node=o→a→t. 7. Explore (1,2)='a': path='oath', Trie node=o→a→t→h (word found). Add 'oath', remove from Trie. 8. From (1,0)='e': path='e', Trie node=e. Explore (0,0)='o': path='eo' not in Trie. Explore (2,0)='i': path='ei' not. Explore (1,1)='t': path='eat', Trie node=e→a→t (word found). Add 'eat', remove from Trie. 9. 'pea' not found (no 'p'), 'rain' not found (path issues). 10. Final: ['oath','eat'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Trie Lookup and Pruning
ladders.desc: Depth-first search on the board that checks Trie nodes during traversal and prunes when words are found.
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS function that explores the board recursively while interacting with the Trie. The intuition is that by traversing the board and simultaneously walking down the Trie, we can efficiently check if the current path corresponds to any dictionary word prefixes, and when a complete word is found, we can prune it to avoid duplicates. The algorithm: 1) Start at a cell (i,j) with current Trie node. 2) Check if current node marks a word; if yes, add to results and remove word marker (prune). 3) Mark cell as visited. 4) For each of 4 directions (up, down, left, right), if neighbor is within bounds and not visited, and if neighbor character exists as child in current Trie node, recursively call DFS with that child node. 5) After exploring all directions, unmark visited (backtrack). 6) After recursion, if current Trie node has no children and is not a word, prune it by removing from parent (optimization). Time complexity for this subroutine is O(4^L) in worst case as it explores up to 4 branches per depth L. Space is O(L) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 as the main traversal engine. Common mistakes: forgetting to check bounds, not handling visited cells correctly, missing the pruning step after recursion, and not checking Trie node existence before recursing. Real code pattern: function dfs(i, j, node): if node.word: add to results, node.word=False; mark visited; for dirs: ni,nj=i+dx,j+dy; if in bounds and not visited and node.children[board[ni][nj]]: dfs(ni,nj,node.children[char]); unmark visited; if node no children and not word: delete node.

examples.input: Start at (0,0)='a' with Trie root→a→b(word), board 2x2 [['a','b'],['c','d']]
examples.trace: 1. At (0,0), node=root→a (not word). 2. Mark (0,0) visited. 3. Check neighbor (0,1)='b': node a has child b, so recurse to (0,1) with node a→b. 4. At (0,1), node a→b is word, so add 'ab' to results, set node.word=False. 5. Mark (0,1) visited. 6. Check neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1)='d' not child of b, (0,2) out of bounds, (-1,1) out. 7. Backtrack: unmark (0,1). 8. Return to (0,0): unmark visited. 9. After recursion, node a→b has no children and is not word (since we removed word marker), so prune b from node a. 10. Node a now has no children and not word, so prune a from root.

examples.input: Start at (1,0)='e' with Trie root→e→a→t(word), board from example, visited clear
examples.trace: 1. At (1,0)='e', node=root→e (not word). 2. Mark visited. 3. Check neighbor (0,0)='o': not child of e, skip. 4. Check (2,0)='i': not child. 5. Check (1,1)='t': e has child a? Wait, e→a→t, so e has child a, not t. Actually, from 'e', need 'a' next. So (1,1)='t' not matching. 6. Check (1,-1) out. 7. No valid moves, backtrack. Actually, to find 'eat', need path e→a→t. From (1,0), explore (0,0)='o' no, (2,0)='i' no, (1,1)='t' no, (1,-1) out. So 'eat' not found from here? Correction: In full board, 'eat' is found from (1,0) by going to (0,0)? No, that's 'o'. Let's trace correctly: From (1,0)='e', node=e. Explore (0,0)='o' no. Explore (2,0)='i' no. Explore (1,1)='t' no (need 'a'). Explore (1,-1) out. So no find. Actually 'eat' found from (1,0) by going to (1,1)='t'? That doesn't match 'a'. In example, 'eat' is found via path: (1,0)='e' → (0,0)='o'? That's 'eo'. Wait, in full problem trace, 'eat' was found from (1,0) by going to (1,1)='t' then (0,1)='a'? That's 'eta'. Let's simplify: Assume board [['e','a'],['t','x']], start at (0,0)='e' with Trie e→a→t. Then: 1. (0,0)='e', node=e. 2. Mark. 3. Explore (0,1)='a': node e has child a, recurse to (0,1) with node e→a. 4. At (0,1), node e→a not word. Mark. 5. Explore (1,1)='x' not child. 6. Explore (0,2) out. 7. Explore (-1,1) out. 8. Explore (1,0)='t': node a has child t, recurse to (1,0) with node e→a→t. 9. At (1,0), node is word, add 'eat', prune. 10. Backtrack, prune nodes.

testCases.input: Start (0,0), board [['a']], Trie with 'a', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a','b']], Trie with 'ab', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a','b']], Trie with 'ac', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a','a'],['a','a']], Trie with 'aaa', visited empty
Start (1,1), board [['a','b'],['c','d']], Trie with 'cd', visited empty
testCases.expected: Found 'cd' from (1,1)→(1,0)? Actually (1,1)='d', need 'c' first. So no if start at d.

testCases.input: Start (0,0), board [['x','y']], Trie with 'xy', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a','b']], Trie with 'aba', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a','b'],['c','d']], Trie with 'ab','ac','bd', visited empty
testCases.expected: Found 'ab' and prune, others not from this start

testCases.input: Start (0,0), board [['a','a']], Trie with 'aa', visited empty
Start (0,0), board [['a']], Trie with 'b', visited empty

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie Pruning to Avoid Duplicates and Reduce Search
ladders.desc: The technique of removing found words and empty nodes from the Trie during search to optimize performance.
ladders.explanation: This is the central optimization insight: by modifying the Trie during traversal, we can prevent redundant work. The intuition is that once a word is found, we don't need to find it again, so we can remove it from the Trie. Additionally, if a Trie node has no children after removal, it cannot lead to any new words, so we prune it to reduce future searches. The algorithm: 1) When a word is found at a Trie node, mark it as non-word (e.g., set a flag to False) rather than physically deleting, to avoid breaking ongoing traversals. 2) After processing all children in DFS, check if the current node has no children and is not a word; if so, remove it from its parent (e.g., delete the key). 3) This pruning happens recursively upward. Time complexity benefit is it reduces the Trie size dynamically, potentially cutting off many future search branches. Space complexity remains O(T) but T decreases. This feeds into L1 as the optimization applied during DFS. Common mistakes: pruning nodes that are still in use by other search paths (solved by pruning only after recursion), not handling the root node specially, and incorrectly managing word markers. Real code pattern: In DFS, after recursing to children, if node.children empty and not node.is_word: del parent.children[char] where parent is passed down.

examples.input: Trie with words 'cat', 'car', 'card'. Found 'cat' during search.
examples.output: Trie after pruning: 'cat' removed, node 't' pruned if no other words.
examples.trace: 1. Initial Trie: root→c→a→t(word), also c→a→r(word) and c→a→r→d(word). 2. When 'cat' found, set node t.is_word=False. 3. After processing, node t has no children and is not word, so prune t from node a. 4. Node a now has child r only. 5. If later 'car' found, set r.is_word=False, but r has child d, so not pruned. 6. If 'card' found, set d.is_word=False, d has no children, prune d from r. 7. Now r has no children and not word, prune r from a. 8. a has no children and not word, prune a from c. 9. c has no children and not word, prune c from root.

examples.output: Trie after: 'aa' removed, but 'a' and 'aaa' remain if paths exist.
examples.trace: 1. Trie: root→a(word)→a(word)→a(word). 2. Found 'aa' at second a: set is_word=False. 3. After, second a has child a (for 'aaa'), so not pruned. 4. Structure remains: a(word)→a(not word)→a(word). 5. If 'a' found, set first a.is_word=False, but it has child, so not pruned. 6. If 'aaa' found, set third a.is_word=False, it has no children, prune from second a. 7. Second a now no children and not word, prune from first a. 8. First a no children and not word, prune from root.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure for Prefix Tracking
ladders.desc: Implementing a Trie (prefix tree) to store dictionary words and support prefix checks during board traversal.
ladders.explanation: A Trie is a tree-like data structure where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes spell out words. The intuition is that it allows efficient prefix matching: we can quickly check if a sequence of characters is a prefix of any dictionary word by traversing down the Trie. For the word search problem, this enables us to abandon board paths early if they don't match any prefix. The algorithm: 1) Define a TrieNode class with children (dictionary mapping char to TrieNode) and is_word flag. 2) Insert each word from dictionary: start at root, for each character, create child node if not exists, move to it, at end set is_word=True. 3) Search for a word: traverse characters, return is_word at end. 4) Check prefix: traverse characters, return True if path exists. Time complexity for insert is O(L) per word, search O(L). Space is O(T) where T is total characters. This feeds into L2 as the foundation for pruning. Common mistakes: not initializing children properly, forgetting is_word flag, handling case sensitivity, and not supporting Unicode if needed. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children={}; self.is_word=False. Insert: node=root; for ch in word: if ch not in node.children: node.children[ch]=TrieNode(); node=node.children[ch]; node.is_word=True.

examples.output: Trie structure with nodes for c-a-t, c-a-r, c-a-r-d.
examples.trace: 1. Start with root TrieNode. 2. Insert 'cat': root has no child 'c', create node for 'c'. Move to 'c' node. 'c' has no child 'a', create 'a' node. Move to 'a'. 'a' has no child 't', create 't' node. Move to 't'. Set t.is_word=True. 3. Insert 'car': root has child 'c', move to 'c'. 'c' has child 'a', move to 'a'. 'a' has no child 'r', create 'r' node. Move to 'r'. Set r.is_word=True. 4. Insert 'card': root→c→a→r exists. Move to 'r'. 'r' has no child 'd', create 'd' node. Move to 'd'. Set d.is_word=True. 5. Final: root.children['c']→children['a']→children has 't' (word) and 'r' (word)→children['d'] (word).

examples.output: Chain of nodes a→a→a with word flags at each level.
examples.trace: 1. Insert 'a': root has no 'a', create 'a' node, set is_word=True. 2. Insert 'aa': root has 'a', move to 'a'. 'a' has no child 'a', create second 'a' node, set is_word=True. 3. Insert 'aaa': root→a→a exists, move to second 'a'. It has no child 'a', create third 'a' node, set is_word=True. 4. Structure: root→a(word)→a(word)→a(word).

testCases.expected: Trie with three separate branches from root
Trie: t-e-s-t word, with t child for 'tests'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Backtracking DFS on a Grid
ladders.desc: Depth-first search with backtracking to explore all contiguous paths in a 2D grid.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental traversal technique for exploring all possible paths in a grid. The intuition is that to find words formed by adjacent cells, we need to explore every possible sequence starting from each cell, which is naturally done with DFS and backtracking. The algorithm: 1) Define a recursive function that takes current cell (i,j) and current path. 2) Mark the cell as visited (e.g., with a visited matrix or modifying board temporarily). 3) Explore up to 4 neighbors (up, down, left, right) that are within bounds and not visited. 4) For each neighbor, recurse. 5) After exploring all neighbors, unmark the cell (backtrack) to allow other paths to use it. Time complexity in worst case is O(4^L) for a path of length L, as each step can branch 4 ways. Space is O(L) for recursion stack plus O(m×n) for visited if using matrix. This feeds into L3 as the traversal method used with Trie. Common mistakes: not checking bounds, forgetting to unmark visited, using mutable default arguments for path, and stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: def dfs(i,j,visited,path): if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or visited[i][j]: return; visited[i][j]=True; path.append(board[i][j]); # process path; for dx,dy in directions: dfs(i+dx,j+dy,visited,path); visited[i][j]=False; path.pop().

examples.input: 2x2 board [['a','b'],['c','d']], start at (0,0), explore all paths up to length 2.
examples.output: Paths: 'a', 'ab', 'ac', 'abc', 'acb' etc., but limited by length.
examples.trace: 1. Start (0,0)='a', mark visited, path=['a']. 2. Neighbors: (0,1)='b' not visited, recurse to (0,1). 3. At (0,1), mark visited, path=['a','b']. Length 2, stop if max length 2. Backtrack: unmark (0,1), path pop to ['a']. 4. From (0,0), next neighbor (1,0)='c', recurse. 5. At (1,0), mark, path=['a','c']. Backtrack. 6. No more neighbors for (0,0), unmark, return. 7. Also start from other cells similarly.

examples.input: 1x3 board [['x','y','z']], start at (0,0), explore all paths.
examples.trace: 1. (0,0)='x', mark, path=['x']. 2. Neighbors: (0,1)='y' in bounds, recurse. 3. (0,1) mark, path=['x','y']. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2)='z' not, recurse. 4. (0,2) mark, path=['x','y','z']. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (0,3) out, so backtrack. 5. Back to (0,1): also neighbor (0,0) visited, so backtrack to (0,0). 6. From (0,0), neighbor (0,2)='z' (skip (0,1) already done in other order), recurse. 7. (0,2) mark, path=['x','z']. Neighbors: (0,1)='y' not visited, recurse. 8. (0,1) mark, path=['x','z','y']. Backtrack etc.

testCases.input: Board [['x']], start (0,0), visited all true initially
Board [['a','b']], start (0,0), restrict to right moves only

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Understanding Tries and Prefix Trees
ladders.desc: Introduction to Trie data structure for storing and retrieving strings.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: representing a grid or board as a 2D list (matrix) and accessing its cells and neighbors. The intuition is that a word search board is a rectangular grid where each cell has a character, and we need to move to adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically) to form words. The algorithm: 1) Represent board as list of lists, e.g., board = [['a','b'],['c','d']] where board[i][j] gives character at row i, column j. 2) Dimensions: m = len(board) rows, n = len(board[0]) columns. 3) Access neighbors: for a cell (i,j), the four adjacent cells are (i-1,j) up, (i+1,j) down, (i,j-1) left, (i,j+1) right. 4) Check bounds: ensure 0 <= i < m and 0 <= j < n before accessing. Time and space are trivial O(1) for access. This feeds into L4 as the data structure for DFS. Common mistakes: mixing up rows and columns, off-by-one errors in bounds, assuming square board, and not handling empty boards. Real code pattern: m=len(board); n=len(board[0]); directions = [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj=i+di,j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n: # access board[ni][nj].

examples.output: Value 'a', neighbors: (1,0)='d', (0,1)='b', others out of bounds.
examples.trace: 1. Board has 2 rows (m=2) and 3 columns (n=3). 2. Cell (0,0) is row 0, column 0, value board[0][0]='a'. 3. Check up: (0-1,0)=(-1,0) out of bounds (row <0). 4. Down: (0+1,0)=(1,0) within bounds, value board[1][0]='d'. 5. Left: (0,0-1)=(0,-1) out (col <0). 6. Right: (0,0+1)=(0,1) within, value board[0][1]='b'. 7. So neighbors are 'd' and 'b'.
1. m=1, n=1. 2. Cell (0,0) value 'x'. 3. Up: (-1,0) row out. 4. Down: (1,0) row out. 5. Left: (0,-1) col out. 6. Right: (0,1) col out. 7. All neighbors out of bounds.

testCases.expected: No board, error or handle empty
